children.name: Anhima
children.description: A monotypic genus distinguished by a long, thin horn-like spike projecting forward from the forehead. These large, bulky waterfowl inhabit tropical wetlands of South America.

children.name: Anhima cornuta
children.description: Easily identified by the unique, slender white horn on its forehead, which is actually a feather structure that grows continuously. It inhabits the Amazon and Orinoco river basins, favouring marshes and lake edges. Its piercing, far-carrying call is one of the most characteristic sounds of Neotropical wetlands.

children.name: Chauna
children.description: Geese-like screamers with a short crest and a prominent spur on the leading edge of each wing used in combat. They are highly territorial and mate for life.

children.name: Chauna chavaria
children.description: Restricted to the wetlands of northern Colombia and northwestern Venezuela, this large, grey-bodied screamer sports a short black crest and a greyish-white cheek patch. It is a powerful swimmer and a clumsy walker, spending most of its time in shallow water grazing on aquatic vegetation.

children.name: Chauna torquata
children.description: The most widespread screamer, found across central and southern Brazil, Paraguay, Uruguay and northern Argentina. It has a distinctive black ring around its neck and a low, fleshy wattle at the base of its bill. Its loud, trumpet-like duets are used to defend territories across vast marshlands and flooded grasslands.
